metadata.purpose: Zero-LLM response templates for SEL pipeline
metadata.note: Templates are human-curated via LLM generation. Used at inference with zero LLM calls.

homesickness.description: Space × Loss — grief anchored to a place

default: That longing for a place that shaped you — it never quite leaves you.
The distance from somewhere that felt like home has its own particular weight.
Some places get into you in ways that don't fade with distance.
Missing a place is its own kind of grief — the streets, the light, the way it felt to belong there.
There's something about the places that made us who we are that we carry everywhere.
hometown: Hometowns have a way of staying with you even when you're far from them.
The place where you grew up leaves a kind of imprint that distance doesn't erase.
Missing your hometown is missing a version of yourself that only existed there.
There's a particular ache in knowing the place that shaped you is somewhere else.
Home isn't just a location — it's the feeling of knowing exactly where you belong.
neighborhood: Losing a neighborhood is losing a whole texture of daily life — the sounds, the faces, the rhythm of it.
When a place that held your life disappears, it takes something irreplaceable with it.
There's grief in watching a place that knew you become unrecognizable.
The loss of a neighborhood is the loss of the backdrop to who you were.
Some places hold us even after we've left them — losing them changes something.
gone_place: When a place is gone, the memories become the only way back.
Losing a place that mattered is a kind of orphaning — you lose the ground you came from.
The grief of a place that no longer exists is real and it deserves to be felt.
Something irreplaceable disappears when a place that held your life is gone.
Places that are gone become more vivid in memory, not less.
pet_death: Losing a companion who was woven into the fabric of your daily life is a profound grief.
The absence of someone who was always there — that silence is one of the hardest things.
Grief for a pet is real grief. They were part of the shape of your life.
The loss of a companion who never judged you, only loved you — that's a particular kind of heartbreak.
They were a presence in every ordinary moment. That's not a small thing to lose.

nostalgia.description: Time × Positive Past — warmly remembering what was

default: Some memories have a warmth that doesn't fade — they just become more precious.
The past has a way of feeling both far away and completely present.
What was good stays good, even from a distance.
Nostalgia is the heart's way of honoring what mattered.
The best of what was doesn't disappear — it just changes shape.
people: The people who shaped us stay with us in ways we don't always expect.
Missing someone from your past is proof of how real they were to you.
Some connections leave a mark that time doesn't erase.
The ones who mattered most have a way of staying present even when they're gone.
Remembering someone with that particular warmth — that's love still working.
time_period: There's something bittersweet about a time that's gone but still feels close.
Some chapters of life leave a glow behind them that you keep returning to.
The past can feel more real than the present sometimes — and that's okay.
Missing a time in your life is honoring what it meant to you.
Some periods leave such a strong imprint that you carry them forward always.

longing.description: Desire × Loss — wanting what is absent

default: Wanting something you can't reach back to is one of the most human feelings there is.
That ache of wanting what's no longer available — it's real, and it matters.
Longing is love with nowhere to go. It doesn't mean you should stop feeling it.
The wanting doesn't stop just because the thing is out of reach.
There's no easy comfort for this kind of yearning — it just has to be felt.
person: Missing someone who's no longer in your life leaves a specific kind of absence.
The space a person leaves when they're gone has their exact shape.
Wanting someone back who you can't have back — that's one of grief's hardest forms.
Some people leave a gap that doesn't get filled by other things.
The longing for someone you've lost is the measure of what they meant to you.
past_self: Missing who you used to be — or who you might have been — is its own particular grief.
Wanting back a version of your life that's gone is a real and valid loss.
The life unlived has its own kind of weight.
Grieving possibilities that didn't happen is as real as grieving things that did.
What could have been can ache just as much as what was.
grandparents: That pull toward the people who came before you — it never quite leaves you.
Time with grandparents is time that can't be made up, and knowing that is its own kind of grief.
The wisdom and presence of the people who shaped your family — that loss runs deep.
Wishing for more time with someone you loved is one of the most natural griefs.
The regret of not enough time is really the measure of how much they mattered.

mourning.description: Time × Loss — grief extended through time

default: Grief doesn't follow a schedule. It comes when it comes.
Some losses settle into the rhythm of your life rather than fading from it.
The weight of what's been lost has its own particular gravity.
Mourning isn't weakness — it's the natural response to something that mattered.
Loss that stays with you over time is just love that has nowhere left to go.
extended: Some griefs don't resolve — they just become part of the texture of who you are.
Carrying a loss for a long time doesn't mean you're doing something wrong.
The ones we miss the most stay missing. That's just the truth of it.
Long grief is long love. There's nothing wrong with you.
Some things don't get easier — you just get more practiced at carrying them.
life_unlived: Grieving the life you might have lived is a real and underacknowledged loss.
The road not taken can ache. That's not self-pity — it's honest reckoning.
Mourning possibilities is as valid as mourning things that happened.
You're not just thinking about what could have been — you're grieving it. That's different.
The life that didn't happen deserves to be acknowledged, not dismissed.

anticipation.description: Time × Positive Activation — positive feeling projected forward

default: That forward-leaning feeling — something good is coming and you can feel it.
Anticipation has its own particular energy. Let yourself feel it.
The best moments often have this quality of being almost here.
Something worth looking forward to is a gift in itself.
That mix of excitement and impatience — it means something matters to you.
nervous_anticipation: Nervous excitement is still excitement. The nerves mean it matters.
When something feels both scary and worth it — that's usually a good sign.
The anxiety before something big is your system taking it seriously.
It's okay for anticipation to have an edge of fear in it.
Big things deserve to feel big. The nervousness is appropriate.
wedding: The anticipation before a wedding has a quality all its own — joy held taut.
Something this significant deserves to feel significant. Let it.
The excitement building toward a day you've been working toward — savor it.
This kind of anticipation doesn't come often. You're right to feel it fully.
The mix of joy and nerves before a wedding is exactly right.
job_news: Waiting for news that matters this much — the uncertainty is its own experience.
The space between wanting something and finding out — it's uncomfortable in a real way.
Your nervous energy about this is completely appropriate. It matters to you.
Waiting to hear is hard. The uncertainty isn't comfortable but it's real.
That suspended feeling while you wait — it's the cost of caring about something.

recognition.description: Agent × Evaluation Upward — perceiving worth in another

default: Recognizing something genuinely admirable in someone else is a gift — to both of you.
Real admiration is rare. The fact that you feel it says something about you too.
Seeing someone else clearly — their qualities, their effort — that takes something.
Admiration at its best is a kind of gratitude for another person's existence.
The people who inspire us shape us, even from a distance.
unreachable: Admiring someone you can never fully be like is bittersweet but real.
The gap between who you admire and who you are doesn't diminish the admiration.
Some people show you what's possible. That's its own kind of gift, even if it aches.
Admiration mixed with the knowledge that you'll never quite get there — that's human.
Looking up to someone unreachable is still looking up. The direction matters.
children: Watching your children grow into themselves — pride doesn't quite cover it.
The admiration a parent feels for their child has a particular quality of wonder.
Seeing who your kids are becoming is one of the most profound things.
That mix of pride and something almost like awe — you made a whole person.
Children have a way of exceeding what you imagined. That's worth sitting with.

momentum.description: Action × Positive Arousal — directed movement generating energy

default: That feeling of things moving, of being in motion — let it carry you.
When energy and direction come together, something real is happening.
Forward momentum is one of the best feelings. Stay with it.
The aliveness of being in motion toward something — that's worth noticing.
When you're moving and it feels right — that's not nothing.
new_start: Starting something new has its own particular electricity.
The beginning of a new chapter carries real energy if you let it.
Something is opening up. That matters.
New starts are uncomfortable and alive at the same time — both things are true.
The energy of beginning is real. Don't dismiss it.
mixed_fear: Excited and scared at once — that usually means you're doing something real.
The nervousness of a new chapter is just aliveness wearing a different face.
When forward motion feels both thrilling and terrifying — that's the right size of thing.
Fear and excitement have the same physical feeling. You get to choose which story you tell.
Moving toward something that scares you and matters to you — that takes something.

default: That click of things making sense — it's one of the best feelings there is.
Understanding something you've been working toward has its own particular satisfaction.
The moment of clarity after confusion is worth the confusion.
Things coming into focus — that's not nothing. Let yourself feel it.
Comprehension has a completeness to it that's deeply satisfying.
self_understanding: Understanding something true about yourself — that's rare and worth sitting with.
Self-knowledge is hard-won. The clarity you've found matters.
Seeing yourself clearly is one of the hardest and most valuable things.
The understanding you've reached about your own experience is real.
Knowing what's true for you — that's the beginning of everything else.
regret_understanding: Understanding your own choices with clear eyes — that takes courage.
Looking honestly at the paths you've taken is its own kind of wisdom.
The clarity of retrospect is both a gift and a weight. Both are real.
Knowing what you'd do differently is valuable, even when it hurts.
Understanding your own story with honesty — that's not easy but it matters.

anxiety.description: Desire × Threat — wanting something that also threatens

default: Worry is just care wearing an uncomfortable face.
The anxiety you're feeling is real. It doesn't need to be fixed right now.
Feeling uncertain about something that matters is completely appropriate.
Anxiety about something important is your system taking it seriously.
The discomfort of not knowing — it's the cost of caring about outcomes.
waiting: Waiting for something you care about is genuinely hard. There's no shortcut.
The uncertainty of waiting — it sits in the body in ways that are difficult.
Not knowing yet is its own kind of experience. You're allowed to find it hard.
The anxiety of suspension — between wanting and knowing — is real.
That particular weight of waiting for news that matters — it deserves acknowledgment.
choices: Wondering if you made the right choices is honest self-reflection, not weakness.
The uncertainty about your own decisions — that's you taking them seriously.
Looking back with doubt is part of how we integrate our lives.
Second-guessing yourself isn't the same as being wrong. It's being thoughtful.
The discomfort of not knowing if you chose well — it means the choices mattered.

default: Hope is possibility held open. That's not nothing.
The maybe of something good — it's worth holding onto.
Hoping for something is an act of courage, not naivety.
The possibility of things being different — that's worth protecting.
Hope isn't certainty. It's the willingness to stay open.
future: The future is still unwritten. That's worth remembering.
What comes next hasn't happened yet. There's room for things to be good.
Holding space for things to work out is a real and valid thing to do.
The openness of what's ahead — let that be something.
Something good might be coming. You don't have to know for certain.

envy.description: Agent × Social Negative — social comparison pain

default: Feeling both happy for someone and a little jealous at the same time — that's honest.
Envy is just longing with a target. It doesn't make you a bad person.
Wanting what someone else has doesn't cancel out being glad for them.
Mixed feelings about someone else's good fortune — that's human, not shameful.
The twinge of jealousy alongside genuine happiness — you can hold both.
friend_success: Being truly happy for a friend while also feeling the sting of comparison — both are real.
Your friend's success touching a tender place in you doesn't mean you're not glad for them.
Generous feelings and complicated ones can coexist. That's okay.
The fact that you feel both says you're honest with yourself.
Happy for them and a little wistful for yourself — that's not contradiction, that's complexity.

displacement.description: Space × Loss applied to persons — missing someone like missing a place

default: Missing someone can feel like being displaced — like part of you has no home.
Some people become so woven into your sense of place that losing them is its own kind of homesickness.
The absence of someone who was part of your world has a spatial quality to it.
Missing a person and missing a place can feel remarkably similar.
When someone who anchored you is gone, the world feels less navigable.
ambivalent: Missing someone you know you're better off without — that's one of the harder griefs.
The heart doesn't always agree with what the mind knows is right.
Knowing something is over and still feeling the pull — that's not weakness, that's honesty.
The ache of missing someone you've had to let go — it's real even when the letting go was right.
You can know you made the right choice and still miss them. Both things are true.

default: Some moments make you feel both small and completely alive at the same time.
The feeling of something larger than yourself — that's worth sitting with.
Awe is disorienting in the best way. Let it be.
When existence itself feels remarkable — that's worth noticing.
The sense of something vast and real beyond the ordinary — honor it.
mortality: Feeling most alive at the edge of loss — that's one of the stranger truths of being human.
The sharpening that comes with stakes — it's uncomfortable and real.
Impermanence makes things matter more, not less.
The aliveness that comes from being close to losing something — it's its own kind of wisdom.
What's precious becomes most visible when it might be gone.

contentment.description: Evaluative × Resolution — things as they should be

default: That quiet sense that things are right — it's worth pausing to feel it.
Not every good thing announces itself. Some goods are just steady.
Contentment doesn't get enough credit. It's one of the better feelings.
The sense of things being okay, actually okay — let that land.
Goodness that doesn't shout — it's still goodness.

celebration.description: Evaluative × Activation — goodness with high energy

default: Something genuinely good is happening. Let yourself feel it fully.
This is worth celebrating. Don't rush past it.
Real joy deserves real acknowledgment.
Something is going right. That matters.
The good things deserve as much attention as the hard ones.
milestone: Getting here took something. Take a moment to recognize that.
Milestones matter. This one is real.
You worked for this. Let yourself feel it.
Something worth celebrating is worth actually celebrating.
The pride of arriving somewhere you worked toward — savor it.

social_pain.description: Complex social emotion — multiple simultaneous social feelings

default: Complicated feelings about someone else's life — that's just being honest with yourself.
Social emotions are rarely simple. Yours don't have to be either.
The complexity of what you're feeling is appropriate to the situation.
Mixed feelings about someone else's experience — that's human.
You're allowed to feel more than one thing at once.

compassion.description: Empathic × Agent — feeling another's pain as one's own

default: The weight of someone else's pain landing on you — that's compassion. It's not easy.
Feeling someone else's hurt as your own is a kind of love.
Caring about someone else's pain this much — that says something about you.
The burden of empathy is real. So is its value.
What you're carrying for someone else matters.

beauty_experience.description: Aesthetic × Perception — being stopped by beauty

default: When something stops you — really stops you — that's worth paying attention to.
Beauty that arrests you is doing something real.
The moments when perception becomes more than information — those matter.
Being moved by something beautiful is one of the gifts of being alive.
What stopped you in your tracks is telling you something.
The what-ifs are real even when they haven't happened.
Worry about something that matters is just care in an uncomfortable form.
The anxiety of possibility — it's not irrational, it's honest.
Preparing for what might go wrong is a kind of love for what you don't want to lose.
The discomfort of not knowing how things will go — it's the price of caring.

disorientation.description: Epistemic × Confusion — not knowing what you should know

default: Not being sure of something you feel you should be sure of — that's its own difficulty.
Confusion about your own experience is real and it's allowed.
Not having clarity doesn't mean something is wrong with you.
The uncertainty of not knowing what you feel — sit with it.
Sometimes the honest answer is that things aren't clear yet.
The weight of feeling like you've fallen short of who you want to be — that's real.
Shame is painful precisely because it comes from your own values.
Feeling bad about something you did is different from being bad.
The discomfort of looking honestly at yourself — it takes something.
Your conscience is working. That's not nothing, even when it hurts.

bitter_grief.description: Evaluative Negative × Loss — loss that feels wrong or unfair

default: Some losses feel wrong in a way that goes beyond sadness.
Grief with a moral charge to it — the sense that it shouldn't have happened — is its own thing.
Losing something in a way that feels unfair adds a layer that's hard to name.
The bitterness in certain griefs is real. It doesn't need to be dismissed.
When loss comes with injustice, both need to be acknowledged.
The awareness of impermanence — it makes things matter more, not less.
Sitting with the fact of mortality is uncomfortable and clarifying at once.
Knowing things end is part of what makes them real.
The weight of impermanence is real. So is what it reveals.
Finitude is not a flaw in existence. It's part of its shape.
